summary: Three months after gastric sleeve surgery, Cathy O'Neil reflects on the physical, dietary, and psychological changes she's experienced. She's dropped several clothing sizes and adopted an all-black wardrobe for simplicity. Her relationship with food has fundamentally changed—hunger no longer feels like a panic alarm, and her taste preferences have shifted toward simpler foods. She's started running again for the first time in twelve years, partly to manage anxiety about parenting, politics, and the state of the world. She candidly discusses the temptation of alcohol and the loss of food as a comfort mechanism, while noting that thin people use food for comfort without stigma.
key_points: The surgery changed the nature of hunger itself—from a 'loud panic button' requiring immediate action to a manageable signal allowing time to cook
Taste preferences shifted dramatically, with previously bland foods now tasting rich and sweet/buttery cravings disappearing
She acknowledges the psychological challenge of losing food as a coping mechanism while noting thin people comfort-eat without judgment
Exercise has become necessary for managing anxiety about parenting teenagers, misogyny, and political cruelty
She's wary of alcohol becoming a replacement comfort, noting it 'works really really well' post-surgery
money_quotes: Before, if I got hungry, it was like a loud panic button in my brain had been set off.
Nowadays, I realize I'm getting hungry, but it simply means that I have to start thinking about food pretty soon.
It's hard being a woman in this age of misogyny. It's hard being a human in this age of political cruelty.
I have lost food as a source of comfort.
Before you blame my obesity on that, let me suggest that thin people comfort themselves with food all the time but aren't accused of bad behavior.
Long story short, I'm doing great, and I feel healthy, and the world still is what it is.
key_insight: Weight loss surgery doesn't just change your body—it fundamentally alters your relationship with hunger, comfort, and coping mechanisms, forcing you to find new ways to deal with life's anxieties.
